summary: In 1906, I lived at 1141 West 21st Street in Los Angeles, which was still very much a country town back then. I attended a public kindergarten at the Norwood Street School and later a private German kindergarten in the city. My family physician, Dr. Charles W. Fish, who lived nearby, even supplied neighbors with milk from his cow. By 1907, we had moved to 1700 South Western Avenue, still in Los Angeles, where I continued my schooling.

I attended the public kindergarten at the Norwood Street School in Los Angeles in 1906. It was one of my first educational experiences before moving on to a private German kindergarten. I remember a particular moment there when we were asked about the origins of everyday items, which led to my innocent answer about milk coming from fishes. This school marked the beginning of my formal education in the city.

After a brief time at the 24th Street School in 1907, my parents enrolled me at Harvard Military Academy, which was conveniently located right across the street from our home on South Western Avenue. I started there in the fourth grade at the age of seven in September 1908 since the academy did not have lower grades. I graduated from grammar school there in June 1913, marking an important phase of my early education.

In the latter half of 1913, from June to December, my mother and I traveled through Europe. Our journey began with a few weeks in London, where we were joined by family members and friends. We toured England, Scotland, and Ireland in August 1913, sharing the experience with the Walkers. This trip was a significant cultural and familial exploration during that year.

During our 1913 European trip, my mother and I spent several weeks in London. There, we were joined by my aunt, Mrs. May Viven, the widow of Captain John L. Viven, as well as the Walkers and their relatives. London served as a central meeting point before we toured other parts of the British Isles. It was a memorable part of our extended family journey that year.

Mrs. May Viven was my father's sister and the widow of Captain John L. Viven, a regular army officer. She joined my mother and me in London during our 1913 European trip. Having her with us added a familial connection to our travels, as we explored England, Scotland, and Ireland together with the Walker family. Her presence helped make the journey more meaningful.

Captain John L. Viven was my uncle by marriage, a regular army officer and the late husband of my aunt, Mrs. May Viven. Although he had passed away before our 1913 trip, his memory was present as my aunt joined us in London during our European travels. His military background connected our family to a tradition of service and discipline.

The Walker family traveled with us during our 1913 tour of England, Scotland, and Ireland. Mr. and Mrs. Walker, along with Mrs. Walker's sister Mrs. Hattie Malloy and their daughter Ethelwyn, accompanied my mother and me. Their company made the trip a shared family adventure, strengthening our bonds across the British Isles. We enjoyed exploring these countries together that summer.

Ethelwyn was the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Walker, who traveled with us during our 1913 European tour. She was part of the group that included her mother’s sister, Mrs. Hattie Malloy, and my family. Traveling together through England, Scotland, and Ireland, Ethelwyn and I shared the experience of exploring these countries as young companions. It was a memorable time for all of us.

Colonel H. Torrens Kenny II was my father's cousin and a distinguished military man who had commanded the Twelfth Lancers in India. During our 1913 trip, we visited him in Folkestone, England. He was fluent in Russian and Hindustani, and I last saw him in 1938 when he visited New York City at age 80. He enjoyed taking me to Russian and Hindu restaurants to showcase his language skills, which left a lasting impression on me.

The Twelfth Lancers was the cavalry regiment in India that my father's cousin, Colonel H. Torrens Kenny II, commanded. His leadership there was part of his distinguished military career. During our 1913 visit to him in Folkestone, England, he shared stories of his time with the regiment. This connection gave me insight into British military history and colonial India.

India figured in my family's history through Colonel H. Torrens Kenny II, who commanded the Twelfth Lancers there. Although I did not visit India myself, my 1913 visit to the Colonel in England included hearing about his experiences in India. His knowledge of Hindustani and his stories enriched my understanding of the region and its culture. India was thus connected to my family through military service and language.

I last saw Colonel H. Torrens Kenny II in New York City in 1938 during one of his annual visits. By then, he was 80 years old but still eager to share his linguistic talents by taking me to Russian and Hindu restaurants. This meeting in New York City was a memorable reunion that connected me again with my family's military and cultural heritage. The city served as a backdrop for this special encounter.

Russia came into my life through Colonel H. Torrens Kenny II, who spoke Russian fluently. During his 1938 visit to New York City, he took me to Russian restaurants to demonstrate his language skills. Although I never traveled to Russia myself, these experiences gave me a glimpse into Russian culture through his stories and culinary introductions. Russia was thus part of my cultural education thanks to the Colonel.

Hinduism entered my experience through Colonel H. Torrens Kenny II, who spoke Hindustani and took me to Hindu restaurants in New York City during his 1938 visit. While I did not study the religion directly, these outings introduced me to aspects of Hindu culture and cuisine. This exposure was part of the Colonel's effort to share his linguistic and cultural knowledge with me.

England was a major part of my 1913 European trip with my mother, where we spent several weeks starting in London. We visited family and friends, including my father's cousin, Colonel H. Torrens Kenny II, in Folkestone. Later that summer, we toured other parts of England along with Scotland and Ireland, accompanied by the Walker family. England was the gateway to a memorable family journey across the British Isles.

In August 1913, during our European tour, we traveled through Scotland with the Walker family. This was part of a broader trip that included England and Ireland, making it a rich cultural experience. Scotland's landscapes and history added depth to our journey, shared among family and close friends. It was an unforgettable part of that summer's travels.

Ireland was one of the countries we toured in August 1913 alongside England and Scotland. Traveling with the Walker family, we explored Ireland's unique culture and scenery as part of our extended family trip. This visit completed our circuit of the British Isles that summer, creating lasting memories. Ireland's charm was a highlight of our European adventure.
